fields.meaning_mnemonic: This radical consists of a single, horizontal stroke. What's the biggest, single, horizontal stroke? That's the ground. Look at the ground, look at this radical, now look at the ground again. Kind of ...
One line plus another line equals two lines. This radical represents the number two. Pretty simple, right? Can you guess what three looks like? I bet you can...
A big, gaping, square hole represents someone opening their giant mouth to tell you something stupid.

Lying on the ground is something that looks just like the ground, the number One. Why is this One lying down? It's been shot by the number two. It's lying there, bleeding out and dying. The number One...
fields.meaning_hint: To remember the meaning of One, imagine yourself there at the scene of the crime. You grab One in your arms, trying to prop it up, trying to hear its last words. Instead, it just splatters some blood ...
fields.reading_mnemonic: As you're sitting there next to One, holding him up, you start feeling a weird sensation all over your skin. From the wound comes a fine powder (obviously coming from the special bullet used to kill O...
fields.reading_hint: Make sure you feel the ridiculously itchy sensation covering your body. It climbs from your hands, where you're holding the number One up, and then goes through your arms, crawls up your neck, goes do...

fields.meaning_mnemonic: The kanji two and the radical two are the exact same things. Therefore, you know the kanji for two already!
fields.meaning_hint: More simply put, you can just know that this kanji is the double of One. It's two lines, that's not all that hard to remember!
fields.reading_mnemonic: Think of all the things you have Two of. What is the most important? Some would say eyes, some would say ears. I would say the knee (に) because if you didn't have them, it'd be really hard to go up st...
fields.reading_hint: Imagine yourself without two knees, and see what it would be like. What would give you trouble? What couldn't you do? Just imagining this for a minute or two will help you to remember the reading of t...

fields.meaning_mnemonic: This kanji is made up of the ground radical (which also happens to be the kanji for "one") and the radical for two. Combine one and two together and what do you get? Three!
fields.meaning_hint: This one should be pretty self explanatory! Even easier, you can look at this kanji and see it has three straight lines. You know what one looks like. You know what two looks like. So... this is three...
fields.reading_mnemonic: Three is the number of chances you get with Santa-san (さん). He has a list, and when you get to three "naughty things" lines, that's it. You're on Santa-san's three strikes and you're out list.
fields.reading_hint: Picture Santa-san sitting at his desk at the North Pole, eating cookies, and marking one, two, then finally three lines next to names. Once the third line appears, the kid doesn't get presents anymore...

fields.meaning_mnemonic: As is the case with most vocab words that consist of a single kanji, this vocab word has the same meaning as the kanji it parallels, which is one.
fields.reading_mnemonic: When a vocab word is all alone and has no okurigana (hiragana attached to kanji) connected to it, it usually uses the kun'yomi reading. Numbers are an exception, however. When a number is all alone, w...
fields.sentences: 一ど、あいましょう。 (Let’s meet up once.))一いはアメリカ人でした。 (First place was an American.))ぼくはせかいで一ばんよわい。 (I’m the weakest (person) in the world.))

fields.meaning_mnemonic: When it's one o'clock it's one o'clock. It's time to eat lunch, or take a nap, or something.
fields.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.
fields.sentences: 毎日一時にランチを食べます。 (I eat lunch at one o'clock every day.))一時までにおもちゃをかたづけなさい。 (Put your toys away by one o'clock.))もし私のところで働くなら、あなたのシフトは午後一時から遅くとも三時までになりますが、よろしいですか？ (If you worked for me, your shift woul...

fields.meaning_mnemonic: This word follows the "number of things" pattern where there's a kanji for a number plus つ on the end. Whenever you see this, you know the word means "____ things." Knowing that, as long as you know t...
fields.reading_mnemonic: Since all of the "number of things" words all follow the same pattern (number plus つ), you really just have to remember the part before the つ (and then remember that the つ is in all of these). All of ...
fields.sentences: こたえを一つえらびます。 (Choose one answer.))お一人さま一つかぎりです。 (It's limited to one per customer.))きのう、一つめこぞうにあった。 (I met a one-eyed goblin yesterday.))

fields.japanese_kana: おはよう　ございます。
おはよう　ございます。

deckName: Genki Kanji - English + Readings to Kanji::Genki Lesson 22 Kanji

fields.Example Sentence: 凄い雨になった。彼はいつもテストで100点を取ります。凄い人です。
fields.Sentence Transliteration: すごい あめ に なった。かれ は いつも てすと で 100 てん を とります。 すごい ひと です。
fields.Sentence Translation: It began raining heavily.He always gets perfect scores on tests. He is an incredible person.
fields.Pitch Accent: ~i+Nすごい~idesuすごいです~kuteすごくてすごくて~kattaすごかったすごかった~kunaiすごくないすごくない~kunakattaすごくなかったすごくなかった~kerebaすごければすごければ~iすごい~kuすごくすごく

fields.Example Sentence: 親切にしてくださってどうもありがとうございます。あの村の人々はとても親切です。
fields.Sentence Transliteration: しんせつ に して くださって どうも ありがとう ございます。あの むら の ひとびと は とても しんせつ です。
fields.Sentence Translation: Thank you for being so kind to me.People in that village are very kind.

fields.Example Sentence: そんなことは絶対できないよ。絶対に駄目です。
fields.Sentence Transliteration: そんな こと は ぜったい できない よ。ぜったい に だめ です。
fields.Sentence Translation: I can never ever do such a thing.Absolutely not.

fields.Example Sentence: あの都市はオリンピックの有力な候補地です。
fields.Sentence Transliteration: あの とし は おりんぴっく の ゆうりょくな こうほ ち です。
fields.Sentence Translation: That city is a strong candidate to host the Olympics.

fields.Example Sentence: その日、国民は平和への祈りをささげるの。
fields.Sentence Transliteration: その ひ、こくみん は へいわ へ の いのり を ささげる の。
fields.Sentence Translation: People pray for peace on that day.

fields.Example Sentence: 私の秘書はとても優秀です。
fields.Sentence Transliteration: わたし の ひしょ は とても ゆうしゅう です。

fields.RemarksFront: Note that the Japanese "ha"-character (は) is pronounced "wa" in this case.
fields.RemarksBack: konnichiha: hello; good day; good afternoonThere are many more greetings in Japanese, see next cards or the reference for a summary (I didn't find a shorter vid - sorry).

fields.RemarksFront: The combination of "o" and "u" is usually pronounced as long "o". In Japanese, this can result from various character combinations (yo/よ + u/う in this example), don't worry about this for now.
Again note that the Japanese "ha"-character (は) is pronounced "wa" in this case.

fields.Content: When これ　それ　あれ are followed by a noun, we replace the れ by の.They follow the same pattern of こ　そ　あ, ordered by proximity.The difference between これ and この is that この directly describes what comes direct...
fields.Expression1: この 飲み物[のみもの]は 何[なん]ですか？
fields.Expression2: この 動物[どうぶつ]は 猫[ねこ]です。
fields.Expression3: この 時計[とけい]は 高[たか]いですか。
fields.TabsBunpro: このthisこの + NounNoun near the speaker
fields.TabsNHK15:
Demonstratives (Lesson 3)

      Demonstratives are the words we use, when we point to the things, 
persons or places we are talking about. We call them KO-SO-A-DO words, 
taking the first syllables o...
fields.References: Bunpro: この Genki I: Lesson 02-2-このそのあのどのJPOD101: Lesson 257NHK15: Lesson 03

fields.Cloze: 毎日同じ物を食[...not こと]は、面白くない。
fields.Expression: 毎日同じ物を食べるのは、面白くない。
fields.Kana: まいにちおなじものをたべるのは、おもしろくない。
fields.English: It's not interesting to eat same thing every day.
fields.Notes: 126 Basic Grammar - Noun-related Particles: The 「の」 particle
fields.Reading: 毎日[まいにち] 同[おな]じ 物[もの]を 食[た]べるのは、 面白[おもしろ]くない。

fields.Notes: 096 Basic Grammar - Transative and Intransitive Verbs: Pay attention to particles!
fields.Reading: 電気[でんき]が 点[つ]いた。

fields.Cloze: ボブは、魚が好きなんだ[...you know, dontcha?]。／そうだね。
fields.Expression: ボブは、魚が好きなんだよね。／そうだね。
fields.Kana: ボブは、さかながすきなんだよね。／そうだね。
fields.English: You know Bob, you like fish, dontcha? / That is so, huh?
fields.Notes: 149 Basic Grammar - Using Adverbs and Gobi: Combining both to get 「よね」
fields.Reading: ボブは、 魚[さかな]が 好[す]きなんだよね。／そうだね。

models.modelName: Japanese Jo Mako's Core Anime Vocabulary

fields.Expression: おい　なんかいうことは
fields.English: Hey, do you have anything to say for yourself?
fields.Reading: おい　なんかいうことは

fields.Expression: でも　１人ではないのなら…
fields.English: But if I'm not doing it alone...
fields.Reading: でも　１ 人[ひとり]ではないのなら…
fields.Definition: 【でも】 but, however, though, nevertheless, still, yet, even so, also, as well
いる 【居る】to be (of animate objects), to exist

fields.Question: How to express present (non-past) state of being (is, will be) for nouns and na-adjectives?
fields.Answer: noun/na-adj + だ. The だ could also be emmited but with it the sentence is more forcefull\declarative.
fields.Examples: 人だ - is person学生だ - is student元気だ - is well

fields.Question: How to express present (non-past) negative state of being (is not) for nouns and na-adjectives?
fields.Examples: 人じゃない - is not person学生じゃない - is not student元気じゃない - is not well

fields.Question: How to express past state of being (was) for nouns and na-adjectives?
fields.Examples: 人だった - was person学生だった - was student元気だった - was well

deckName: Tae Kim's Guide to Japanese Grammar

models.modelName: Tae Kim's Guide to Grammar MIA

fields.Explanation: One of the trickiest part of Japanese is that there is no verb for the state-of-being like the verb “to be” in English. You can, however, declare what something is by attaching the Hiragana character ...
You can say you’re doing well or someone is a student without using 「だ」 at all.
fields.Expression1: Ａ 「 元気[げんき] ？ 」Ｂ 「 元気[げんき] 。 」

fields.Explanation: In Japanese, negative and past tense are all expressed by conjugation. We can conjugate a noun or adjective to either its negative or past tense to say that something is not [X] or that something was ...
fields.Expression1: 学生[がくせい] じゃない[;n] 。
fields.Expression2: 友達[ともだち] じゃない[;n] 。
fields.Expression3: 元気[げんき] じゃない[;n] 。
